transcript.clean_text: Welcome to highlights of stage 7, right here on GCN Racing. Yesterday, it was another summit finish, and another breakaway win for a 2nd division team. This time, it was Jesus Herrada who delivered the goods for Cofidis, his first Grand Tour stage win. 7 seconds behind him was Dylan Teuns, who could be equally content, as he’d done enough to ride himself into the race lead and therefore the red jersey. Today, we’ve had yet another hilltop finish. A 183km route to the Mas de la Costa, a climb which averages over 12% for the final 4kms, with a maximum of 18%. Another big test for the GC contenders. Of which there are less by the day. Further to the withdrawal of Uran, Carthy, Roche and De La Parte yesterday, Davide Formolo was also unable to take to the start today due to the injuries he sustained in the same crash. We had well over an hour of furious attacks at the start of the stage before a group was eventually able to prise itself clear. Jelle Wallays , Philippe Gilbert , Sergio Henao , Stéphane Rossetto , Quentin Jauregui , Gianluca Brambilla , Michael Storer and Sebastian Henao , Barthe, Marcynski. Brambilla the best placed on GC at a little over 8 minutes. By this point, EF Education First had lost yet another rider, Tejay van Garderen also unable to make it to the finish of the stage due to his injuries from his separate crash yesterday. Today, too, wasn’t without it’s incidents. Here, a couple of riders of BH Burgos hit the deck whilst trying to pace KOM leader and stage winner Angel Madrazo back to the bunch. And as they were making their way back on - more problems, this time for James Knox of Deceuninck Quickstep. A very high tempo was being set in the bunch by Movistar, we can presume for Valverde. Quintana was quoted in the press today as saying that the World Champion was the sole leader of the team. On the penultimate climb, Marzcynski, a double stage winner here at the Vuelta two years ago, was pressing hard on the pedals, marked closely by Gilbert. It would put an end to the chances of Rossetto and Jauregui. Towards the top, we were down to three - Gilbert now with Sergio Henao and Brambilla. The pace being set behind was enough to put paid to the hopes of race leader Dylan Teuns, who’d been distanced - we’d be getting the 4th change in red jersey in as many days. Up front, the lead group had decimated, The riders from the original break break scattered across the climb. This man has had a tough time of it so far - Tao Geoghehan hart came into the race as an outside for a good GC position, but illness scuppered those plans. He looks to be getting better, though, he attacked the bunch on the climb. At the top, it was Henao who’d take maximum points, his cousin Sebastien having just managed to get back on. With Movistar continuing to set a blistering pace, it wouldn’t be long before Geoghehan hart was brought back, but up front, Gilbert was on a flyer. On an unclassified climb, he’d distance all the others and set of solo. Coming towards the foot of the final wall, Astana were back on the front controlling things, the gap down to 45 seconds, and another half a minute had been chopped off that by the time Gilbert and Henao started the climb proper. It wouldn’t be long before the gradient begun to bite. Ouch, that’s DAni Navarro of Katusha Alpecin, yet another rider to have hit the deck. A few hundred metres later and the leading duo were caught - today would be won by one of the GC contenders, and Jumbo Visma were clearly confident in the chances of their main man, Primoz Roglic. Here’s Sepp Kuss, who was on another flyer today, setting the early tempo and putting a lot of riders into trouble. Next, it was George Bennett, the group already down to just a dozen or so riders. But then the big names came to the front - Nairo Quintana, working for himself, or for Valverde? To me, it looked like the latter - this was not an attack, this was Quintana setting a hard pace for the World champion. And so, we were soon down to 4, the four strongest riders in the race. Quintana, Roglic, Lopez and Valverde. The quartet were glued to each other midway up the climb, nothing to separate them, but plenty of separation between them and the rest. Next rider on the road was Rafal Majka, Pogocar, Chaves, Aru and Izaguirre all further down the road. Still great to see Aru fighting towards the front again, he hasn’t got the nicest of styles on the bike, and it’s been a tough couple of years for the Italian, but he appears to be getting back towards his best. At their best, were these 4. Things literally couldn’t have been any closer between them with just 600m to go, but there can only be one winner, and someone that’s been a proven winner for a long time is Valverde. There’s no one better on steep climbs like this when they’re on form, and at 39, the World Champion was proving that there is still plenty of power left in the legs. He hit out with 200m to go and noone was able to respond. Roglic would come home 2nd, on the same time, whilst it was a further 6 seconds before the Colombian duo of Lopez and Quintana crossed the line. Best of the rest was Majka, but it had all closed up behind, he’d come to the line just ahead of Izaguirre, with Pogocar and Aru of UAE just behind that. Tomorrow, slightly less hilly, amazingly, although this is not necessarily a sprinters stage. A 7km climb peaks with 27kms to go, it’ll be interested to see whether any of the sprinters teams attempt to control it, or whether this is one for a breakaway. My prediction, See you tomorrow.
